source: Learning Ansible with Rocky - Ansistrano Deployments: Introduction: what Ansistrano is, its steps and layout

options: An Ansible role to easily deploy applications (PHP, Python, ...)
A standalone CLI tool independent of Ansible
questions.why: Ansistrano is an Ansible role for application deployment.

questions.q: Ansistrano is based on the functionality of which tool?
questions.why: It reproduces Capistrano's deployment approach as an Ansible role.

questions.q: What does Ansistrano require to run?
options: Ansible on the deployment machine, plus rsync or git on the client
questions.why: Ansible on the deploy machine; rsync or git on the managed client.

questions.q: From where can Ansistrano fetch the source code?
options: rsync, git, scp, http, S3, and more
questions.why: It supports several methods: rsync, git, scp, http, S3, etc.

questions.q: Which of these is NOT one of Ansistrano's five deployment steps?
questions.why: The five steps are Setup, Update Code, Symlink Shared, Symlink, and Clean Up -- there is no Compile step.

questions.q: What does the Setup step do?
options: Creates the directory structure that will host the releases
questions.why: Setup builds the directory structure to hold releases.

questions.q: In the release layout, what is the `current` symlink?
options: A symbolic link pointing to the active release under releases/
questions.why: current -> ./releases/<timestamp> points at the live release.

questions.q: What is the purpose of the `shared` directory?
options: It holds files/folders kept across releases, symlinked into each release
questions.why: shared/ content is symlinked into each release so it persists between deployments.
